race.name: Etape Caledonia
race.tagline: Scotland's premier closed-road sportive. 80 miles through Highland Perthshire in the shadow of Schiehallion — the fairy hill where they first weighed the Earth.

vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed stations with water, energy food, and mechanical support

climate.primary: Scottish Highlands spring — cool, wet, unpredictable
climate.description: May in Highland Perthshire means 45-60F with the genuine possibility of rain, hail, wind, and sunshine within the same hour. Scottish weather doesn't follow forecasts — it follows its own logic. Exposed sections over moorland and along lochs amplify wind chill. Hypothermia is a real risk on cold, wet days. The best days are stunning; the worst are survival exercises.
challenges: Rain and wind are probable, not possible — pack accordingly
Temperature range of 40-60F with wind chill dropping lower on exposed sections
Hypothermia risk if wet and cold on moorland stretches
Four-season weather in a single ride is standard Scottish operating procedure

terrain.primary: Paved Scottish Highland roads with sustained rolling climbs
terrain.surface: Paved B-roads and A-roads through Highland Perthshire. Roads are narrower than continental European sportive routes. Surface quality is generally good but varies. Closed to traffic on event day.
features: Schiehallion — the conical 'fairy hill' dominates the landscape (backdrop, not climbed directly)
Glen Lyon — Scotland's longest glen, with a sustained climb out
Tummel Valley — loch-side riding with rolling terrain
Several sustained climbs of 3-8% gradient, none exceeding HC category
Closed roads: the entire course is traffic-free on race day

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 56. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 54.

course_description.character: Rolling Highland roads through glen and loch scenery. The route loops through Highland Perthshire with Schiehallion's conical peak as the visual anchor. Climbs are sustained but moderate — 3-8% gradients through wooded glens and across open moorland. Descents are fast on narrow Scottish roads. The character shifts between sheltered glen riding and exposed moorland sections where wind and weather hit hard. It's a course that rewards steady riding and punishes impatience.
course_description.signature_challenge: Glen Lyon — the sustained climb out of Scotland's longest glen on tired legs, with 40 miles already ridden and the knowledge that the exposed moorland crossing still awaits.

logistics.airport: Edinburgh (EDI) — 90 minutes. Glasgow (GLA) — 2 hours. Both well-connected to European and some transatlantic routes.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Pitlochry has hotels, B&Bs, and guesthouses but capacity is limited for 5,000+ riders. Book 3-6 months ahead. Aberfeldy and Blair Atholl are nearby alternatives. Camping available at local sites.
logistics.food: Pitlochry has a decent selection of pubs, cafes, and restaurants for a Highland town. The post-ride food at the event village is included.

history.origin_story: Launched in 2007, the Etape Caledonia established itself as Scotland's premier mass-participation cycling event. The closed-road format — unusual in the UK where most sportives share roads with traffic — immediately set it apart. The route through Highland Perthshire, circling Schiehallion and threading through Glen Lyon, showcases some of Scotland's most dramatic landscape. It quickly grew to 5,000+ riders and became the anchor of the Scottish cycling calendar.
notable_moments: 2007: First edition draws thousands to Pitlochry for Scotland's biggest closed-road sportive
The event established the template for premium UK closed-road sportives
Route passes Schiehallion — where Nevil Maskelyne's 1774 experiment first measured Earth's density
Glen Lyon, Scotland's longest glen, became synonymous with the event
history.reputation: Scotland's cycling showpiece. The closed-road format and Highland scenery make it a bucket-list UK sportive.

biased_opinion.summary: The Etape Caledonia is Scotland's cycling crown jewel — 80 closed-road miles through Highland Perthshire with Schiehallion as your constant companion. The closed-road format is rare in Britain and transforms the experience from 'sportive with traffic anxiety' to 'proper European-style riding.' The scenery is world-class: glens, lochs, ancient mountains, and the kind of light that only Scotland delivers. The climbing is honest without being savage. The one variable you can't control is weather — and Scottish weather in May is a dice roll that can deliver paradise or purgatory with equal probability.
strengths: Closed roads: 80 miles of traffic-free Highland riding — a premium feature in UK cycling
Highland Perthshire scenery: Schiehallion, Glen Lyon, and Loch Tummel provide genuine visual drama
Value: excellent entry fee for a closed-road event of this scale
5,000+ riders create mass-participation energy without overcrowding
weaknesses: Scottish weather is a genuine gamble — cold rain turns the experience from sublime to miserable
No international prestige: this is a UK event, not a global destination sportive
Pitlochry accommodation fills fast — the town is small and the event is large
Climbing is moderate: if you want Alpine severity, this isn't it
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Britain's best closed-road sportive through Scotland's most beautiful terrain. Pack a rain jacket. Then pack another one.

final_verdict.one_liner: 80 closed-road miles through Highland Perthshire. Scotland's premier sportive in the shadow of the fairy hill.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want Britain's best closed-road sportive experience. Yes if Highland scenery matters more than Alpine climbing. Skip if you can't handle the genuine possibility of cold rain for 6 hours.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want closed roads with bigger climbs: try the Etape du Tour. If you want UK cycling with more edge: try the Fred Whitton Challenge. If you want Scottish cycling with more terrain: try the Etape Royale.

youtube_data.search_query: Etape Caledonia gran fondo cycling Pitlochry

videos.title: Etape Caledonia 2021 Sportive in Scotland
videos.description:
On todays adventure...
85 miles of closed road cycling beginning in Pitlochry. The course consists of rolling hills and forest-lined roads around Loch Rannoch and Loch Tummel, combined with the demanding, twisting climb of Mt Schiehallion. 

I'll be riding with
Altaf, Sham and the Mearns CC.

Ride Stats:
85 miles
4,400ft elevation
Mission objective don't BONK!

Thanks for watching, Beard on a Bike.

🔔 SUBSCRIBE FOR MORE - https://www.youtube.com/c/BeardonaBike/

F O L L O W
▶ YouTube - https://www.youtube.com/c/BeardonaBike/
▶ Instagram - https://www.instagram.com/a_beard_on_a_Bike/
▶ Strava - https://www.strava.com/athletes/8538430

Equipment:
▶ Summer bike: Specialized Tarmac Expert Disc
▶ Winter bike: Cannondale Caad13 105 Disc

My Camera Kit:
▶ Gopro Hero 7
▶ Samsung Galaxy S10+
▶ Canon 80d
▶ Sony RX100 V

Beard on a Bike logo by:
https://www.jenniferbates.co.uk/

🎵 Song: Freestyler
🎧 Artist: Aries Beats

videos.curation_reason: First-person race recap covering the full 85-mile Etape Caledonia experience including navigation challenges, course conditions, and the Mt Schiehallion climb.

videos.title: Etape Pennines 2013 - Start (Part 1)
videos.description: The start of the 2013 Etape Pennines event, our first road cycling sportive.  A great event, the staff, participants and locals were all brilliant.

videos.title: National Cycle Network Route 7 Balloch Croftamie Endrick Water Drymen Guide
videos.description:
Part of a series covering cycling in the Highlands of Scotland - Sustrans National Cycle Network Route 7 Lochs and Glens North (Glasgow - Inverness). Watch for new sections as they get added. The routes are all done as return trips so you can see the conditions and scenery in both directions and are intended to give you a feel for the route, gradient and surface - as well as showcasing the beautiful scenery.

A beautiful evening run, Balloch to Drymen – the shortest stage on this Route 7 series , approx 10 miles from the Leven Bridge – and chasing the sunset back to Balloch and Loch Lomond on the return leg.

It’s a short ride a couple of hours of cycling time, ideal for an evening. The stage overlaps the John Muir way for a lot of its length.

Loch Lomond and Balloch are only 10m above sea level so we are starting low, and almost immediately have a 50m climb up through the Balloch Castle Country park – that’s the biggest  and longest climb. We then  drop a few metres down to the main Stirling road  before another short climb to about 80m and a very gradual downhill for a few miles  to the  bridge over the Endrick Water  at about  15m and then finish with a short sharp climb back up towards Drymen and a final gentle downhill and flat section ending at about 55m.

It’s minor road and footway/cycle track, roughly 70/30 proportions. No rough stuff at all and some of the road sections were getting resurfaced. The roads have little traffic and have  generally good visibility. There are 3 main road crossings with fast traffic.

The pipe bridge/walkway over the Endrick Water near Croftamie is a highlight of the route - once a railway bridge its just a pipeline now with a narrow and high metal walkway on top.  It’s approx. 50m long, just 1m wide (98cm inside clearance). I’m not good with heights, but for me it was fine – not being able to see the ground (it sits on top of a pipe) through the grating helps, so no problem crossing. You might prefer to walk across – especially if on a road bike and you are worried about your tyres. The sides are high enough, but when sitting on a saddle you might feel a bit exposed, especially if windy. It’s a nice spot.

Cycled with a Specialized Tricross with 32c Marathon Plus tyres.
GoPro Hero 4 Silver, 1080 wide, protune, gopro colour -0.5 ev to retain the skies using the Grad Filter effect in https://youtu.be/5W3QNmbGs98

Additional material Lumix TZ30 set at -2/3 ev
Edited with Sony Movie Studio Platinum 13. No stabilisation (it cuts the field of view). Some Youtube compression artifacts after upload owing to the lack of stabilisation lots of detail and not enough bits to go round. 

Royalty-free music by Underwaterbeats and Riot (courtesy of You Tube Music Library)

videos.title: 99% Of Cyclists Make This Mistake When Buying A New Bike
videos.description:
Stop! Don't make this bike buying mistake. Getting the wrong size bike is a common mistake, one that we've all made. It can lead to some important health issues, such as Shermer's neck, knee pain and numb hands. Luckily we have a solution! Selle Italia's ID match technology will fit you perfectly to any bike. If you're looking to purchase your next dream bike, you've got to do this first. 

Find out more about ID Match here 👉 https://gcntech.co/selleidmatch 

In association with 👉 @selleitaliachannel 

00:00 Welcome
00:42 What is Selle Italia ID Match
01:53 Using A Bike Fit To Get The Right Bike
06:00 Having A Bike Fit For Endurance Riding

Brought to you by the world’s biggest cycling channel, the Global Cycling Network (GCN), GCN Tech is the only channel you need for all things bike tech – past, present and future.

Simply put, we’re obsessed with tech: we seek out and showcase the best in bikes, components, tech, accessories, upgrades and more from races and events, tech shows and product launches across the globe to bring you the best in road bike technology.

We’ve also got great maintenance videos to help you get the most from your bike; pro-bike tours from all the biggest races; special features and the weekly GCN Tech Show. We also take a deeper look into the future of cycling, apps, smart tech and virtual riding. 

Join us on the channel and the GCN App to submit your content, vote on the latest tech and keep abreast of exciting new trends.

problem was is that that then exposed
problems I have within my fit and it's
made me realize if I was to do an ultra
event a long endurance thing again I
would go much more relaxed so I'm going
to ask the machine to use its algorithms
and AI to put me in what it reckons
would be a much better um endurance
position and let's see how that differs
to this redoing the bike fit with the AI
set for a more relaxed endurance
position changed the fit but not how I
thought I should also point out that
while the AI experiments with different
changes this is overseen by a human bike
fitter who can also listen to me and
make human tweaks if required 10 minutes
later we had a result so've done the
endurance fit Hayden what's what's the
system spitting out now we're going to
look at your handlebar stack and reach
now it's primarily changed your
handlebar stack it's not made any
adjustments to your handlebar reach so
what we're looking to do or what the
software is let to do there is basically
increase your your handlebar height
which is then in turn effectively going
to take some pressure off your neck also
it may shift some of the weight from
your hands onto your saddle slightly and
give you slightly more of kind of offset
weight distribution yeah in doing that
whilst keeping your same hand while
reach should help maintain your kind of
nice relaxed posture on the bike but
take some of the weight off your hands
and take some pressure off your neck
it's a bit counterintuitive to me though
cuz I would have thought that um when
you go more endurance like people go oh
you coming shorter and higher but it's
not you're saying it's not changed the
rid kept that good like the same yeah
that's not always the case cuz sometimes
what we find is if you if you pull your
reach shorter so if you maintain the
same stack hype that just made the bars
closer to you it yeah It ultimately
tucks you up it can change your posture
on your bike and it can also cause you
to round your back too much and what you
can get to there is is a more tension
through your neck because you have to
lift your head slightly more so you get
end up with a much more acute neck angle
well that's I had that problem so there
you have it thanks to VZ for showing us
the system I really hope you found this
insightful because people spend a
fortune you know when they get a serious
bike and they obsess over the details of
it and and all the bits on it but often
don't take seriously enough the most
fundamental thing which is the fit and
how you sit on it and I think if you if
you want to see an example of just how

quotes.text: 85 miles of closed road cycling beginning in Pitlochry. The course consists of rolling hills and forest-lined roads around Loch Rannoch and Loch Tummel, combined with the demanding, twisting climb of Mt Schiehallion.
It's the morning of the etap caledonia mayhem so yesterday we came here and everybody's like this road's closed half five that's it it's now quarter to seven the traffic's still coming up and down.

key_challenges.name: Bridge over the Endrick Water
key_challenges.description: Narrow metal walkway on top of a former railway bridge. One rider described being worried about this section due to fear of heights and noted the metal grating might be sharp for tires. However, after crossing, found it wider than expected with good high sides and solid feel.

key_challenges.name: Balloch Castle Country Park climb
key_challenges.description: 50m climb that's described as the biggest and longest climb on the route, starting almost immediately from the 10m elevation start point.

key_challenges.name: Navigation challenges
key_challenges.description: Riders report getting lost easily, with one group riding seven miles in the wrong direction. Signs require close attention as the route isn't always obvious.

key_challenges.name: Three main road crossings
key_challenges.description: Riders note three main road crossings as potential hazards along the route.

terrain_notes.text: Route follows the John Muir Way for much of its length with gradual elevation changes. Starts at about 10m above sea level, climbs to 80m, then gradually descends to 15m at the bridge before finishing at about 55m elevation.
Very wet conditions reported during the event with morning mist around the loch areas.

race_day_tips.text: Start very early (around 6am) but bring lights as it will be dark for navigation to the start line.
Pay close attention to route signs as the way isn't always obvious and it's easy to get lost.
Be prepared for potential food station shortages - riders reported no gels or energy tablets at some feed stations.

additional_quotes.text: It's the morning of the etap caledonia mayhem so yesterday we came here and everybody's like this road's closed half five that's it it's now quarter to seven the traffic's still coming up and down
We have mechanical support for riders who might need any assistance. Couple of brake adjustments a couple of puncture repairs

rider_intel.search_text: The Etape Caledonia in Pitlochry presents riders with navigation challenges and varied terrain across its 80-mile route. The course follows the John Muir Way for much of its length, starting near sea level and featuring the biggest climb through Balloch Castle Country Park - a 50m ascent early in the route. A key challenge is the narrow bridge over the Endrick Water at mile 15, a former railway bridge with metal walkway that initially appears intimidating but proves manageable. Riders report very wet conditions with morning mist around loch areas. Navigation requires close attention to signage as the route isn't always obvious, with some riders going seven miles off course. The race starts very early around 6am in darkness, requiring lights for navigation to the start line. Road closures can be inconsistent with traffic still present after supposed closure times. Feed stations may have supply issues, with reports of missing gels and energy tablets. Mechanical support is available along the route for brake adjustments and puncture repairs. Three main road crossings present additional hazards along the Scottish Highlands course.
